title: Coffee on Cotton
stainChemistry: Coffee contains tannins (polyphenolic compounds from roasted beans) that bond to cotton through hydrogen bonding and pi-stacking interactions. Fresh coffee is water-soluble. Aged stains oxidize and darken — the brown color intensifies as tannin molecules cross-link. Melanoidins from the roasting process add additional brown pigment that is highly resistant to removal.
whyThisWorks: An acidic tannin spotter (professional) or oxidizer (DIY) breaks the chromophore bonds — the colored structures — in the tannin molecules on cotton. Hydrogen peroxide specifically targets the double bonds that make oxidized tannin brown. The key is avoiding heat and alkaline products, both of which permanently darken tannin stains.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center — 5-10 min dwell time
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min — 5-15 min dwell time
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin — 2-3 min dwell time
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min dwell time
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If coffee stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is coffee on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test on hidden area first to ensure fabric tolerates the solutions.
Dab the coffee stain immediately with cool water applied to a white cloth. Work from center outward to prevent spreading.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Coffee contains tannins that oxidize and turn brown — act quickly.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply detergent/vinegar solution (1/4 tbsp mild laundry detergent + 2 oz white vinegar + 2 oz cool water) to the stain. Rub gently with the back of a spoon from center outward.
Rinse thoroughly with cool water, working from center outward.
Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% mixed with ammonia (equal parts) and allow to sit for 3 minutes to oxidize tannin residue.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Test on hidden area first — peroxide may lighten some dyes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply white vinegar to neutralize and remove any rings. Rinse again with cool water. Wipe rings outward from center with white cloth.
If stain persists or if fabric is delicate, call a professional cleaner.
